title: Autonomous Attackers: AI Agents in Pentesting

modules.title: The Agentic Pentesting Landscape: Current Tools, Capabilities, and Limitations
modules.sub: Mapping today's AI agent tools and their real-world boundaries
modules.description: Learners survey the current ecosystem of AI-driven pentesting tools, from autonomous scanners to LLM-backed exploitation frameworks, and assess what each can and cannot reliably accomplish. They evaluate capability claims critically, distinguishing marketing hype from demonstrated performance in controlled conditions. By the end, learners can produce a structured comparison of available agentic tools relevant to their engagement contexts.

modules.title: Planning and Reasoning Loops: How Agents Navigate Reconnaissance and Exploitation
modules.sub: Understanding how agents plan, adapt, and chain attack steps
modules.description: Learners trace the internal reasoning cycles that AI agents use to prioritize targets, select tools, and pivot based on intermediate findings during a simulated engagement. They analyze how planning loops handle uncertainty, dead ends, and unexpected responses from target systems. This understanding allows learners to predict agent behavior, anticipate failure modes, and intervene at the right moments.

modules.title: Integrating AI Agents into Structured Pentest Methodologies (PTES, OWASP, MITRE ATT&CK)
modules.sub: Fitting agentic tools into recognized frameworks and workflows
modules.description: Learners map AI agent capabilities onto established methodologies such as PTES phases, OWASP testing guides, and MITRE ATT&CK tactics to identify where automation adds the most value and where it creates gaps. They practice inserting agent-assisted steps into a documented engagement plan while maintaining methodology compliance. The result is a hybrid workflow template that satisfies both operational efficiency and professional reporting standards.

modules.title: Human-in-the-Loop Design: When to Delegate, When to Override
modules.sub: Defining safe delegation boundaries and critical override triggers
modules.description: Learners design decision checkpoints within an agentic workflow that determine which actions the agent executes autonomously and which require explicit human authorization before proceeding. They work through scenario-based exercises involving ambiguous scope boundaries, high-impact exploit opportunities, and unexpected data exposure to practice override judgments. Learners leave with a personal escalation policy they can adapt to real engagements.

modules.title: Detecting and Defending Against Agentic Attack Patterns in Your Own Environment
modules.sub: Recognizing automated campaign signatures defenders can act on
modules.description: Learners analyze log samples, network traces, and endpoint telemetry generated by AI-augmented attack simulations to identify the behavioral fingerprints that distinguish agentic activity from manual testing or routine traffic. They configure detection rules and alerting logic tuned to the speed, sequencing, and volume anomalies characteristic of autonomous campaigns. By the end, learners have a working detection playbook deployable in their monitored environments.

modules.title: Ethical, Legal, and Scope Constraints for Autonomous Testing Engagements
modules.sub: Navigating authorization, liability, and responsible use guardrails
modules.description: Learners examine the legal frameworks, rules of engagement, and professional ethics standards that govern autonomous testing, including how expanded agent autonomy can inadvertently breach scope or trigger unauthorized access statutes. They review real-world incident cases where automation outpaced authorization and identify the contractual and technical safeguards that could have prevented harm. Learners draft a pre-engagement checklist that addresses the unique risk surface introduced by agentic tools.

modules.title: Hands-On Lab: Running and Evaluating an AI-Augmented Pentest Workflow
modules.sub: Executing, monitoring, and critiquing a full agentic engagement scenario
modules.description: Learners execute a scoped AI-augmented pentest against a purpose-built lab environment, operating the agent through reconnaissance, vulnerability identification, and controlled exploitation phases while enforcing the human-in-the-loop policies defined earlier in the course. They capture agent decision logs, evaluate the quality and completeness of autonomous findings against manual verification, and document gaps requiring human judgment. The lab concludes with a structured debrief report that demonstrates readiness to deploy agentic tools responsibly in professional engagements.

synopsis: AI agents can now autonomously chain reconnaissance, vulnerability identification, and exploitation steps — compressing multi-day engagements into hours and raising the capability floor for both legitimate testers and malicious actors. This course equips penetration testers and red teamers to responsibly deploy agentic tools and defenders to recognize the behavioral signatures these automated campaigns leave behind.
rationale: Agentic offensive tooling is moving from research labs into commercial and open-source frameworks faster than defensive detection logic and professional ethics standards can keep pace.
learning_outcome: Learners can integrate AI agents into a scoped pentest engagement, identify where human oversight is non-negotiable, and configure behavioral detections to catch agentic attack patterns in monitored environments.
source_signals: Comparing AI agents to cybersecurity professionals in real-world pen testing
Small models also found the vulnerabilities that Mythos found
AI cybersecurity is not proof of work
